title: Japan chases its World Cup dream despite injury setbacks | NHK WORLD-JAPAN News
original_text:
In the men's soccer World Cup, Japan has made a strong start to the challenge of securing a place in the club of the global elite sides, with a draw against a group favorite and a decisive win. But some key players were unable to take part in the 2026 tournament due to injury and the national side has had difficulties to overcome.

Japan faced a very strong opponent, the Netherlands, in their first Group F match. Going into the game, Netherlands was eighth in the FIFA world rankings, while Japan was 18th. The Dutch side has finished runner-up in the World Cup three times.

Japan conceded the opening goal to the Netherlands in the 51st minute, but equalized just six minutes later through Nakamura Keito.

Then in the 64th minute, the Netherlands scored again to go ahead until Japan dramatically produced an equalizer from a corner kick in the 89th minute.

In their second game, Japan beat 45th-ranked Tunisia, 4-0, in what was the 1,000th match in World Cup history.

Two of the goals came from Ueda Ayase, who scored 25 times for Feyenoord this season to be the top scorer in the Dutch league's Eredivisie.

Japan also made history, as it was the first time the team had scored four goals in a World Cup match and the first time the national team had not conceded a goal in a game since 2014.

Japan entered this World Cup having stunned the world with its performance at the previous tournament and with some big scalps since then to underline just how far its team has progressed.

Four years ago in Qatar, Japan defeated four-time champions Germany and one-time winner Spain in the group stage, advancing to the knockout rounds as group winner, a result few had predicted.

Since then, Japan has continued to deliver impressive results in international competition.

In September 2023, they faced Germany again in a friendly match. Germany were looking for revenge, but Japan delivered a convincing 4-1 victory.

Then last October, Japan took on Brazil. Trailing 2-0 at halftime, the Japanese team mounted a massive fightback to win 3-2, including a first goal by Minamino Takumi which changed the course of the game.

In March, less than three months before the World Cup, Japan faced England at Wembley Stadium. England were ranked fourth in the world at the time, but Japan edged them 1-0, with Mitoma Kaoru scoring on a counter attack.

Japan has embarked on this World Cup campaign without several of its key players.

In May, head coach Moriyasu Hajime named Japan's squad, garnering worldwide news coverage.

Mitoma, who plays for Brighton in the English Premier League and has scored nine goals in his 31 appearances for Japan, was not selected because of a hamstring injury.

Meanwhile, Minamino of AS Monaco in France's Ligue 1, was left out because he is still recovering from a serious knee injury.

Speaking about the absence of key players, Moriyasu said: “Both the fans who support us and our opponents recognize Mitoma as a major presence. I think our opponents may feel that the pressure on them has eased a little. For us, it is a major blow. But we have worked until now to make sure the team can function no matter which players are on the pitch. We want to show that we can win with the overall strength of the team.”

But injuries continued to undermine Japan's preparations until just days before the opening to their World Cup campaign.

Endo Wataru, a midfielder for English Premier League side Liverpool and Japan's captain, was at the team's training base in Tennessee when he withdrew from the squad. He had been aiming to recover from an injury to his left foot, but his condition did not improve.

Then in Japan's opening match against the Netherlands, Kubo Takefusa, who had been central to the team's attack, had to go off with knee injury after a challenge from an opponent. He missed the game against Tunisia.

Itakura Kou was appointed captain in place of Endo Wataru.

When Endo's withdrawal was announced, Itakura had tears in his eyes as he called on the players to come together as one team: "Wataru has led this team since the last World Cup and brought us this far. I think he must be feeling extremely frustrated, and I think everyone understands how he feels. Let's once again remember what it means to represent Japan, to fight with responsibility, and let's win this tournament."

Stories such as this have repeatedly been reported in Japan from traditional outlets to social media, serving to deepen many people's sense of attachment to their national team.

Public screening of games across Japan have attracted large crowds of cheering fans.

Hailed as the strongest Japanese side ever, the team's fortunes are not only followed by Japanese fans but admirers from across the wider soccer world.

Japan's final game in the group stage is against Sweden and the fans are looking forward to seeing how far Japan can progress in this World Cup even without some of its key players.
_note: 全文走 summary 块A(LLM三判后只增不改);processed=成熟仓库累积,raw=本次收集过程。
